function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Annualize Q1 2026 consolidated revenue and Connectivity revenue and compare each run-rate to FY2025. What does the implied growth profile suggest about the concentration of consolidated momentum?

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the financial data I need. I need to find Q1 2026 consolidated revenue, Q1 2026 Connectivity revenue, and FY2025 consolidated revenue to compute annualized run-rates and compare them.

Let me first parse the S-1 filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve the financial data I need. I'll look for Q1 2026 consolidated revenue, FY2025 consolidated revenue, and Connectivity segment revenue for both periods.

arguments.prompt: What are the consolidated total revenues for Q1 2026 and FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Also, what are the Connectivity segment revenues for Q1 2026 and FY2025?

results.content:
Based on the provided documents, here are the requested revenue figures:

*   **Consolidated total revenues for Q1 2026:** **$4,694 million**
*   **Consolidated total revenues for FY2025:** **$18,674 million**
*   **Connectivity segment revenues for Q1 2026:** **$3,257 million**
*   **Connectivity segment revenues for FY2025:** **$11,387 million**

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the key data:

- Q1 2026 consolidated revenue: $4,694 million
- FY2025 consolidated revenue: $18,674 million
- Q1 2026 Connectivity revenue: $3,257 million
- FY2025 Connectivity revenue: $11,387 million

Now let me compute:

1. Annualized Q1 2026 consolidated revenue = $4,694M × 4 = $18,776M
2. Compare to FY2025 consolidated revenue of $18,674M
3. Annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity revenue = $3,257M × 4 = $13,028M
4. Compare to FY2025 Connectivity revenue of $11,387M

Then calculate growth rates:

Consolidated: (18,776 / 18,674) - 1 = ?
Connectivity: (13,028 / 11,387) - 1 = ?

Also, let me figure out what the rest of the business (non-Connectivity) looks like:

Non-Connectivity FY2025: 18,674 - 11,387 = 7,287M
Non-Connectivity Q1 2026: 4,694 - 3,257 = 1,437M
Annualized non-Connectivity: 1,437 × 4 = 5,748M
Non-Connectivity growth: (5,748 / 7,287) - 1 = ?

Let me compute all of these.

Also, I should look at what the "Connectivity" segment covers and what the other segments are to understand the concentration dynamics. Let me also get segment-level data for other segments if available.
Now let me compute the non-Connectivity annualized and growth:
Now let me also get the segment breakdown to understand what segments exist beyond Connectivity, and to get more context on the revenue concentration.

arguments.prompt: What are all the operating segments and their revenues for Q1 2026 and FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Show the full segment revenue breakdown.

results.content:
The company has three reportable operating segments: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**. Below is the full revenue breakdown for the first quarter of 2026 and the fiscal year 2025.

### Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026)

| Segment / Type | Revenue ($ millions) |
|----------------|----------------------|
| **Space** | **619** |
|     Launch Services | 330 |
|     Launch & Development | 289 |
| **Connectivity** | **3,257** |
|     Consumer | 2,148 |
|     Enterprise & Government | 1,109 |
| **AI** | **818** |
|     Advertising | 343 |
|     AI Solutions & Infrastructure | 475 |
| **Total Revenues** | **4,694** |

### FY2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025)

| Segment / Type | Revenue ($ millions) |
|----------------|----------------------|
| **Space** | **4,086** |
|     Launch Services | 2,576 |
|     Launch & Development | 1,510 |
| **Connectivity** | **11,387** |
|     Consumer | 7,208 |
|     Enterprise & Government | 4,179 |
| **AI** | **3,201** |
|     Advertising | 1,844 |
|     AI Solutions & Infrastructure | 1,357 |
| **Total Revenues** | **18,674** |

*Note: All products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment.*

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me compute annualized run-rates for all segments:

Space:
- Q1 2026: $619M → annualized: $619M × 4 = $2,476M
- FY2025: $4,086M
- Growth: (2,476 / 4,086 - 1) × 100

AI:
- Q1 2026: $818M → annualized: $818M × 4 = $3,272M
- FY2025: $3,201M
- Growth: (3,272 / 3,201 - 1) × 100

Connectivity:
- Q1 2026: $3,257M → annualized: $13,028M
- FY2025: $11,387M
- Growth: (13,028 / 11,387 - 1) × 100 = 14.41%

Consolidated:
- Q1 2026: $4,694M → annualized: $18,776M
- FY2025: $18,674M
- Growth: (18,776 / 18,674 - 1) × 100 = 0.55%

Let me compute the Space and AI growth rates.

Now let me compute the revenue shares:

Q1 2026:
- Connectivity: 3,257 / 4,694 = 69.39%
- Space: 619 / 4,694 = 13.19%
- AI: 818 / 4,694 = 17.43%

FY2025:
- Connectivity: 11,387 / 18,674 = 60.98%
- Space: 4,086 / 18,674 = 21.88%
- AI: 3,201 / 18,674 = 17.14%

Now let me also compute the incremental revenue contributions:

Consolidated annualized vs FY2025: 18,776 - 18,674 = $102M incremental

Connectivity annualized vs FY2025: 13,028 - 11,387 = $1,641M incremental
Space annualized vs FY2025: 2,476 - 4,086 = -$1,610M
AI annualized vs FY2025: 3,272 - 3,201 = $71M

So the consolidated growth of $102M is almost entirely explained by Connectivity (+$1,641M), but offset by a sharp decline in Space (-$1,610M). AI is essentially flat (+$71M).

This suggests that consolidated momentum is heavily concentrated in Connectivity. In fact, without Connectivity's growth, the consolidated revenue would be declining significantly. The consolidated headline growth of ~0.5% masks very divergent segment dynamics.

Let me also note that Q1 2026 is only one quarter, and Space revenue can be lumpy due to launch cadence. Let me check if there's any disclosure about this.

arguments.prompt: What does the S-1 disclose about seasonality, quarterly trends, or lumpiness of revenue across segments, particularly for Space launch services, in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
The S-1 MD&A disclosure on Space launch services addresses lumpiness and quarterly trends, though it does not use the word “seasonality.” It indicates that Space revenue is subject to significant **period‑to‑period variability** driven by the mix of launches.

Key points from the MD&A chunk:

* **Quarterly comparison** – The table shows Space revenue composition for the three months ended March 31, 2026 versus March 31, 2025. Launch Services’ share fell from 65.4% to 53.3%, while Launch & Development rose from 34.6% to 46.7%. This demonstrates how the revenue profile can shift from one quarter to another.
* **Lumpiness from launch mix** – The disclosure states: *“From period to period, Space revenue will vary based on the mix of launches used for…”* (followed by a break, but context indicates internal vs. customer launches). Because Launch Services revenue is recognized at a **point in time** (upon launch), it can spike or drop depending on the timing of customer launches, creating lumpiness. In contrast, Launch & Development revenue is recognized **over time** using a cost‑to‑cost method, which tends to be less lumpy but still varies with contract progress.
* **Structural shift** – The S-1 notes that Launch & Development is expected to become a larger portion of Space revenue as the company continues serving long‑term government contracts. This shift toward over‑time recognition could moderate some quarterly lumpiness but does not eliminate it, as total Space revenue still fluctuates with the number and mix of customer launches.
* **Internal launches not recognized** – Launches for Starlink (Connectivity) and, in the future, for the AI segment are not recorded as Space revenue; costs are capitalized. This means reported Space revenue is entirely dependent on external customer activity, making it inherently uneven from quarter to quarter.

Thus, the S-1 clearly warns that Space launch revenue is **not smooth**; it varies substantially from period to period based on the launch manifest and the balance between point‑in‑time and over‑time contracts.

steps.reasoning_content:
This is very helpful context. Now let me put together the full answer.

Key findings:

1. Q1 2026 annualized consolidated revenue: $18,776M vs FY2025: $18,674M → +0.55% growth
2. Q1 2026 annualized Connectivity revenue: $13,028M vs FY2025: $11,387M → +14.41% growth

The implied growth profile shows:
- Consolidated revenue is barely growing at ~0.5%, but this masks extreme divergence:
  - Connectivity is growing at ~14.4% annualized
  - Space is shrinking at ~39.4% annualized
  - AI is growing modestly at ~2.2% annualized

The concentration of consolidated momentum is clear: Connectivity is the sole growth engine. The consolidated run-rate is essentially flat because Connectivity's $1,641M of incremental annualized revenue is almost entirely offset by Space's $1,610M decline.

However, I should note that Q1 may not be representative of the full year, especially for Space which is lumpy. The S-1 explicitly warns about launch revenue variability.

Connectivity's share of total revenue has increased from ~61.0% in FY2025 to ~69.4% in Q1 2026, indicating increasing concentration.

Let me also check if there are any other important details I should include, such as whether the Q1 2026 revenue includes any one-time items.

arguments.prompt: Does the MD&A or financial statements in {{spacex_s1}} discuss any unusual, one-time, or non-recurring items affecting Q1 2026 revenue for any segment?

results.content:
Yes, the MD&A provided in the chunks does discuss an unusual or non-recurring item affecting Q1 2026 revenue. Specifically, within the AI segment’s revenue analysis (which includes X and Grok operations), the narrative highlights that a decrease in advertising revenue was caused by a temporary platform overhaul:

> “The decrease in advertising revenue is due to an overhaul of the Company’s advertising platform which impacted ad sales for a short period of time during the rebuild.”

This overhaul—and its short-term, disruptive impact on ad sales—is presented as a distinct, one-time factor that partially offset the revenue growth from AI solutions and infrastructure. No similarly unusual or non-recurring revenue items are identified for the Connectivity or Space segments in the provided text; their revenue fluctuations are attributed to ongoing trends like subscriber growth, ARPU changes, launch cadence, and government contract timing.

steps.reasoning_content:
Good. So there's a temporary headwind in AI advertising revenue from a platform overhaul. This means AI growth might be understated in Q1 2026.

Now let me put together my complete analysis. Let me also compute some more detailed metrics:

Revenue shares:
- Q1 2026 Connectivity: 69.39%
- FY2025 Connectivity: 60.98%
- Change: +8.41 pp

Incremental revenue (annualized Q1 2026 vs FY2025):
- Connectivity: +$1,641M
- Space: -$1,610M
- AI: +$71M
- Total: +$102M

Connectivity accounts for essentially 100%+ of the net positive consolidated momentum, with Space acting as a major drag.

This is a clear picture of concentration risk: the consolidated revenue trajectory depends almost entirely on Connectivity's continued growth.

Let me now submit the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Annualized Q1 2026 vs. FY2025 Revenue Analysis

### Data (from SpaceX S-1, filed May 20, 2026)

| ($ millions) | Q1 2026 | Q1 2026 Annualized (×4) | FY2025 | Implied Growth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Consolidated** | 4,694 | **18,776** | 18,674 | **+0.55%** |
| **Connectivity** | 3,257 | **13,028** | 11,387 | **+14.41%** |
| Space | 619 | 2,476 | 4,086 | −39.40% |
| AI | 818 | 3,272 | 3,201 | +2.22% |

### Revenue Concentration

| | Q1 2026 Share | FY2025 Share | Change (pp) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity | 69.4% | 61.0% | +8.4 |
| Space | 13.2% | 21.9% | −8.7 |
| AI | 17.4% | 17.1% | +0.3 |

### Implied Growth Profile and Concentration of Momentum

The consolidated annualized run-rate of $18,776 million barely exceeds FY2025 ($18,674 million), implying near-zero headline growth of **+0.55%**. However, this flat aggregate figure is the net result of three sharply diverging segment trajectories:

1. **Connectivity is the sole growth engine.** Annualized Connectivity revenue of $13,028 million represents +$1,641 million in incremental annualized revenue over FY2025 — more than *16×* the total consolidated incremental gain of +$102 million. Connectivity's revenue share expanded from 61.0% to 69.4%, and the segment now accounts for over two-thirds of consolidated revenue.

2. **Space is a substantial and offsetting drag.** Annualized Space revenue of $2,476 million is −$1,610 million below FY2025, nearly erasing Connectivity's entire gain. The S-1 cautions that Space revenue is inherently lumpy — Launch Services revenue is recognized at a point in time (upon launch), and the mix of internal versus external launches creates significant period-to-period variability. Q1 may therefore understate full-year Space revenue, but even with that caveat, the quarter's Space run-rate signals material contraction.

3. **AI is essentially flat.** Annualized AI revenue of $3,272 million is only +$71 million above FY2025 (+2.22%). Moreover, the S-1 discloses that Q1 AI advertising revenue was temporarily depressed by an advertising-platform overhaul — suggesting underlying AI growth may be somewhat stronger than the Q1 run-rate indicates.

### What This Says About Concentration of Momentum

**Consolidated momentum is almost entirely concentrated in Connectivity.** The incremental dollar math is stark:

| Segment | Annualized vs. FY2025 Δ ($M) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity | +1,641 |
| Space | −1,610 |
| AI | +71 |
| **Consolidated** | **+102** |

Connectivity contributes over 100% of net consolidated growth; Space offsets virtually all of it. Absent Connectivity's expansion, the consolidated business would be contracting at a double-digit rate. This extreme concentration means the consolidated revenue trajectory is overwhelmingly a Connectivity story — a single segment's subscriber and ARPU trends dictate the direction of the entire enterprise. While the S-1's Space-lumpiness caveat suggests Q1 may exaggerate the Space decline, the direction and magnitude of the concentration dynamic are unambiguous: Connectivity is not merely the largest segment but the only segment generating material forward momentum as of early 2026.

### Important Filing Context

All figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity under common-control accounting, incorporating xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Consolidated** | 4,694 | **18,776** | 18,674 | **+0.55%** |
| **Connectivity** | 3,257 | **13,028** | 11,387 | **+14.41%** |
| Space | 619 | 2,476 | 4,086 | −39.40% |
| AI | 818 | 3,272 | 3,201 | +2.22% |

### Revenue Concentration

| | Q1 2026 Share | FY2025 Share | Change (pp) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity | 69.4% | 61.0% | +8.4 |
| Space | 13.2% | 21.9% | −8.7 |
| AI | 17.4% | 17.1% | +0.3 |

### Implied Growth Profile and Concentration of Momentum

The consolidated annualized run-rate of $18,776 million barely exceeds FY2025 ($18,674 million), implying near-zero headline growth of **+0.55%**. However, this flat aggregate figure is the net result of three sharply diverging segment trajectories:

1. **Connectivity is the sole growth engine.** Annualized Connectivity revenue of $13,028 million represents +$1,641 million in incremental annualized revenue over FY2025 — more than *16×* the total consolidated incremental gain of +$102 million. Connectivity's revenue share expanded from 61.0% to 69.4%, and the segment now accounts for over two-thirds of consolidated revenue.

2. **Space is a substantial and offsetting drag.** Annualized Space revenue of $2,476 million is −$1,610 million below FY2025, nearly erasing Connectivity's entire gain. The S-1 cautions that Space revenue is inherently lumpy — Launch Services revenue is recognized at a point in time (upon launch), and the mix of internal versus external launches creates significant period-to-period variability. Q1 may therefore understate full-year Space revenue, but even with that caveat, the quarter's Space run-rate signals material contraction.

3. **AI is essentially flat.** Annualized AI revenue of $3,272 million is only +$71 million above FY2025 (+2.22%). Moreover, the S-1 discloses that Q1 AI advertising revenue was temporarily depressed by an advertising-platform overhaul — suggesting underlying AI growth may be somewhat stronger than the Q1 run-rate indicates.

### What This Says About Concentration of Momentum

**Consolidated momentum is almost entirely concentrated in Connectivity.** The incremental dollar math is stark:

| Segment | Annualized vs. FY2025 Δ ($M) |
|---|---|
| Connectivity | +1,641 |
| Space | −1,610 |
| AI | +71 |
| **Consolidated** | **+102** |

Connectivity contributes over 100% of net consolidated growth; Space offsets virtually all of it. Absent Connectivity's expansion, the consolidated business would be contracting at a double-digit rate. This extreme concentration means the consolidated revenue trajectory is overwhelmingly a Connectivity story — a single segment's subscriber and ARPU trends dictate the direction of the entire enterprise. While the S-1's Space-lumpiness caveat suggests Q1 may exaggerate the Space decline, the direction and magnitude of the concentration dynamic are unambiguous: Connectivity is not merely the largest segment but the only segment generating material forward momentum as of early 2026.
